item.title: Does it really make sense to retrain as a plumber?
item.description: It’s become the go-to advice for anyone whose white-collar job could be eliminated by AI — but it’s not very realistic

item.title: The Fed is vacillating as the war continues
item.description: This is starting to look like a central bank appeasing its political masters

item.title: Trump learns that not everyone has a price
item.description: The great cynic did not expect Iran to fight out of conviction

item.title: US investors prefer Europemaxxing to Europebashing
item.description: Venture capital firms know their dollars go a lot further here than in Silicon Valley

item.title: Our euro, your solution
item.description: Reducing the world’s dependency on the dollar is in the interest of every country that buys energy, issues debt or trades

item.title: Political drama reaches heart of UK’s nuclear deterrent
item.description: Captain steps back after his link to Chinese spy case triggers Royal Navy probe

item.title: The disappointing dollar
item.description: And the worst-case scenario for oil

item.title: High street banks could learn from Revolut’s fee bonanza
item.description: Higher account fees would reduce exposure to interest rates for old-school lenders

item.title: The global economy turns out to be more resilient than we had feared
item.description: Trump’s tariffs have not led to the kind of retaliatory action many expected

item.title: Silent cells could mean no more scars
item.description: Scientists believe the key to improving skin regeneration lies in dialling down excessive nerve growth

item.title: Lessons from your petrol pump
item.description: As consumers, sharp price increases are unwanted. But they send an essential signal

item.title: Reasons to be bullish on gold
item.description: The precious metal has suffered a sell-off during the Middle East war but the outlook is more positive

item.title: Ploughshares to swords: a call to arms for industrial Europe
item.description: Carmakers get another chance to revolutionise their production lines amid rearmament push

item.title: Oaktree’s path puts private credit choices into relief
item.description: Market stress to test whether it is right not to pursue scale as aggressively and broadly as some peers

item.title: Can I get money back from my ex-tenants?
item.description: They have taken back their deposit by hacking into the letting agent’s deposit account

item.title: Unilever’s Marmite deal with McCormick looks unpalatable
item.description: Its investors may not have much appetite for exposure to a less tried-and-tested food group

item.title: Price controls make a troubling return to the mainstream
item.description: Governments should focus more on boosting supply and productivity

item.title: The EU doesn’t have an Iran war strategy either
item.description: European leaders blame the US for lacking a long-term plan, but they must slay their own sacred cows to weather this crisis

item.title: What can poetry give us in times of crisis?
item.description: At their best, poets both offer us an immersive break from clamorous news feeds and fortify us for the future

item.title: The ECB’s three-pronged monetary strategy
item.description: Unless the war ends quickly, a rise in Eurozone interest rates looks inevitable

item.title: Trump is not just sinking in the Gulf
item.description: As his poll numbers tank, the president’s trade and immigration agendas are encountering judicial resistance too

item.title: Inflation worries become growth worries
item.description: A longer war makes long bonds interesting

item.title: Why do politicians want AI to go faster?
item.description: Bullish rhetoric about ‘unleashing’ the technology is badly out of touch with the sensibilities of many voters

item.title: In the age of AI, the US must rethink the taxation of labour and capital
item.description: As the population ages and technology disrupts the economy, young workers are carrying a disproportionate tax burden

item.title: In trade’s ‘law of the jungle’, the winners are clear
item.description: It is China and the US that prosper when power sets the terms for global commerce
